viewing-file-contents.label: Viewing File Contents
viewing-file-contents.summary: Inspect text files with cat, head, tail, and wc.

sketch0.title: cat prints a whole text file

sketch1.title: head and tail preview file parts

sketch2.title: wc counts lines, words, and bytes

try_viewing_file_contents_sketch0.title: Read the full message
try_viewing_file_contents_sketch0.prompt: Run `cat notes/message.txt` and read the full message in the terminal.

try_viewing_file_contents_sketch1.title: Preview the beginning and end
try_viewing_file_contents_sketch1.prompt: Run `head notes/story.txt`, then run `tail logs/app.log` to inspect the end of the log.

try_viewing_file_contents_sketch2.title: Count the story lines
try_viewing_file_contents_sketch2.prompt: Run `wc -l notes/story.txt` and look for the line count in the terminal output.

ci-create-log-review.title: Read the full message
ci-create-log-review.prompt:
Run `cat notes/message.txt` and read the full message in the terminal.

Use the terminal. The editor is hidden on purpose for this task.
ci-create-log-review.hint: Use `cat` when you want to print a small text file.

help.concept: This task is checked from the command you run and the text printed in the terminal.
help.hint_2: Check Answer looks for the `cat` command and for `Linux is powerful.` in the output.

ci-create-log-review.placeholder: Use the terminal to complete this task.

ci-create-notes-summary.title: Preview the beginning and end
ci-create-notes-summary.prompt:
Run `head notes/story.txt`, then run `tail logs/app.log` to inspect the end of the log.

Use the terminal. The editor is hidden on purpose for this task.
ci-create-notes-summary.hint: Use `head` for the beginning and `tail` for the ending lines.

help.concept: This task is checked from two commands and the text they print.
help.hint_2: Check Answer looks for the first line of the story and the final line of the log.

ci-create-notes-summary.placeholder: Use the terminal to complete this task.

ci-create-count-marker.title: Count the story lines
ci-create-count-marker.prompt:
Run `wc -l notes/story.txt` and look for the line count in the terminal output.

Use the terminal. The editor is hidden on purpose for this task.
ci-create-count-marker.hint: Use `wc -l` when you only want the number of lines.

help.concept: This task is checked from your terminal command and the line-count output.
help.hint_2: Check Answer looks for a line count of `4` next to `notes/story.txt`.

ci-create-count-marker.placeholder: Use the terminal to complete this task.

cat-shows-whole-file.title: cat prints a whole text file
cat-shows-whole-file.bodyMarkdown:
`cat` prints a file's contents to the terminal.

```bash
cat logs/app.log
```

It is best for small files. Large files can flood the terminal.

head-tail-preview.title: head and tail preview file parts
head-tail-preview.bodyMarkdown:
`head` shows the beginning of a file. `tail` shows the end.

```bash
head logs/app.log
tail logs/app.log
```

This is useful for logs because the newest lines are often at the bottom.

wc-counts.title: wc counts lines, words, and bytes
wc-counts.bodyMarkdown:
`wc` gives simple counts.

```bash
wc notes.txt
wc -l notes.txt
```

`wc -l` is especially useful because it counts lines.
